papers.title: Candidate Hypervelocity Red Clump Stars in the Galactic Bulge Found Using the VVV and Gaia Surveys
papers.author_names: Alonso Luna Dante Minniti Javier Alonso-García

abstract.text: We propose a new way to search for hypervelocity stars in the Galactic bulge, by using red clump (RC) giants, that are good distance indicators. The 2nd Gaia Data Release and the near-IR data from the VISTA Variables in the Via Lactea (VVV) Survey led to the selection of a volume limited sample of 34 bulge RC stars. A search in this combined data set leads to the discovery of seven candidate hypervelocity red clump stars in the Milky Way bulge. Based on this search we estimate the total production rate of hypervelocity RC stars from the central supermassive black hole (SMBH) to be $N_{HVRC} = 3.26 \\\\times 10^{-4} $ yr$^{-1}$. This opens up the possibility of finding larger samples of hypervelocity stars in the Galactic bulge using future surveys, closer to their main production site, if they are originated by interactions of binaries with the central SMBH.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity star surveys or candidates

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper identifies seven candidate hypervelocity red clump (RC) stars in the Galactic bulge using a combined analysis of Gaia DR2 proper motions and VVV near-IR photometry, leveraging RC stars as distance indicators to enhance kinematic detection. By constructing a volume-limited sample of 34 bulge RC stars, they estimate a production rate of 3.26 × 10⁻⁴ yr⁻¹ of hypervelocity RC stars from interactions between binary systems and the central supermassive black hole (SMBH), suggesting a higher detection potential in future surveys targeting the bulge region where SMBH-driven ejections are most likely.

source_fields.title: Candidate Hypervelocity Red Clump Stars in the Galactic Bulge Found Using the VVV and Gaia Surveys

source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: The experiment identifies seven candidate hypervelocity red clump stars in the Galactic bulge with tangential velocities exceeding 7 km s⁻¹ and ejection times spanning the past ∼21,000 years, at a rate of ∼3.26×10⁻⁴ stars yr⁻¹, consistent with theoretical models and suggesting possible discrete ejection episodes from the Galactic center. Conclusions: This study identifies seven bulge red clump star candidates as potential hypervelocity stars ejected from the Galactic center, marking the first possible detection of such stars in the Milky Way bulge and setting a production rate of approximately one every 3000 years, with future surveys poised to significantly expand the sample and refine ejection mechanisms. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel search method using Gaia DR2 and VVV data to identify hypervelocity red clump stars in the Galactic bulge, reporting the discovery of two candidate HVS and addressing the problem of detecting and confirming HVS from the Galactic center's binary-SMBH ejection mechanism. The Gaia and VVV Data: To overcome the challenges of non-uniform reddening and stellar crowding in the Galactic bulge, this study combines Gaia DR2's precise astrometric and photometric data with the extended, high-quality near-IR photometry from the VVV survey. The Selection of Hypervelocity RC stars: This section describes the selection of hypervelocity red clump star candidates in the Galactic bulge using Wesenheit magnitudes to identify red clump stars with accurate positions, photometry, and high proper motions from Gaia and VVV data.

title: Candidate Hypervelocity Red Clump Stars in the Galactic Bulge Found Using the VVV and Gaia Surveys

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title and abstract explicitly state discovery of seven candidate hypervelocity red clump stars from a volume-limited sample of 34 bulge RC stars using Gaia DR2 and VVV data
